title: Authors and Citation
text: Pasche OC, Bodik J (2026). ExtremeGranger: Granger Causality Extremes. R package version 0.1.0, https://opasche.github.io/ExtremeGranger/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {ExtremeGranger: Granger Causality in Extremes},   author = {Olivier C. Pasche and Juraj Bodik},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://opasche.github.io/ExtremeGranger/}, }

title: Granger Causality in Extremes
text: ExtremeGranger R package framework Granger causality extremes, designed identify causal links extreme events observational time series. Granger causality plays pivotal role understanding directional relationships among time-varying variables. task causal discovery time series gains heightened importance extreme highly volatile periods, state---art methods primarily focus causality within body distribution, often overlooking causal mechanisms manifest extreme events. framework designed infer causality mainly extreme events leveraging causal tail coefficient. especially helpful presence hidden confounders. method non-parametric, can handle non-linear high-dimensional time series. methodology introduced Bodik Pasche (2024).

title: Granger Causality in Extremes
text: install latest version ExtremeGranger, R session, run:

title: Granger Causality in Extremes
text: usage example 4-dimensional toy VAR time series lag = 2. can use, example, igraph package plotting estimated graph.
code: # Load the ExtremeGranger R package library(ExtremeGranger)  # Load EnvStats for the example (or any other package that can generate Pareto noise) library(EnvStats)   # Example: Generating a 4-dimensional VAR time series with lag = 2 n <- 5000  set.seed(0) # for reproducibility epsilon_x <- rpareto(n, 1, 1) epsilon_y <- rpareto(n, 1, 1) epsilon_z1 <- rpareto(n, 1, 1) epsilon_z2 <- rpareto(n, 1, 1) x <- rep(0, n) y <- rep(0, n) z1 <- rep(0, n) z2 <- rep(0, n)  for (i in 3:n) {   z1[i] <- 0.5 * z1[i - 1] + epsilon_z1[i]   z2[i] <- 0.5 * z2[i - 1] + epsilon_z2[i]   x[i] <- 0.5 * x[i - 1] + 0.5 * z1[i - 2] + 0.5 * z2[i - 1] + epsilon_x[i]   y[i] <- 0.5 * y[i - 1] + 0.5 * z1[i - 1] + 0.5 * z2[i - 2] + 0.2 * x[i - 1] + epsilon_y[i] }  # Running the extreme causality tests z <- data.frame(z1, z2) result_xy <- Extreme_causality_test(x, y, z, max_causal_lag = 2, p_value_computation = FALSE) result_yx <- Extreme_causality_test(y, x, z, max_causal_lag = 2, p_value_computation = FALSE)  # Estimating the full causality graph w <- data.frame(z1, z2, x, y) G <- Extreme_causality_graph(w, max_causal_lag = 2)  # Try it out also with lag = 1. You will see that the lagged edges disappear # Visualizing the final graph estimate using the igraph package library(igraph)  graph <- graph_from_edgelist(G$G) V(graph)$name <- names(w) plot.igraph(   graph,    layout = layout_nicely(graph),    vertex.label = V(graph)$name,   margin = c(0, 0, 0, 0),   vertex.color = "white",   vertex.label.color = "black",   vertex.size = 30,   edge.color = "black" )

title: Granger Causality in Extremes
text: Bodik, J. Pasche, O. C. (2024). “Granger Causality Extremes.” ArXiv Preprint 2407.09632. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2407.09632.

Granger causality plays pivotal role understanding directional relationships among time-varying variables. task causal discovery time series gains heightened importance extreme highly volatile periods, state---art methods primarily focus causality within body distribution, often overlooking causal mechanisms manifest extreme events. framework designed infer causality mainly extreme events leveraging causal tail coefficient. especially helpful presence hidden confounders. method non-parametric, can handle non-linear high-dimensional time series. methodology introduced Bodik Pasche (2024) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2407.09632 .

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph estimate — Extreme_causality_graph
text: function estimates causal graph (path diagram) set time series. Use Extreme_causality_graph_parallel() instead, faster parallel computations.

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph estimate — Extreme_causality_graph
code: Extreme_causality_graph(   w,   max_causal_lag = 1,   max_confounder_lag = 0,   nu_x = 0.3,   q_y = 0.2,   q_z = 0.1,   instant = FALSE,   both_tails = TRUE,   p_value_based = FALSE,   p_value_cutoff = 0.05 )

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph estimate — Extreme_causality_graph
text: w data.frame time series, numeric length. max_causal_lag time delay effect x y. coefficient 'p' Appendix manuscript. max_confounder_lag lag \(Z\) \((X, Y)\). common cause different lags \(X\) \(Y\), may cause spurious causality \(X\) \(Y\). Ensure max_confounder_lag larger lag. nu_x coefficient \(\tau_X\) \(k_n\) manuscript, defined \(k_n = \lfloor n^{\nu_x} \rfloor\). strong hidden confounding expected, set nu_x 0.4 0.5. q_y coefficient \(\tau_y = q_y \times n\), describing conditioning \(Y_t\). large auto-correlation \(Y\), set q_y 0.1 less. Note manuscript, \(q_y\) defined 1 - q_y. q_z coefficient \(\tau_z = q_z \times n\), describing conditioning \(Z_t\). irrelevant z NULL. strong confounding effects, set q_z 0.2 0.3. Note manuscript, \(q_z\) defined 1 - q_z. instant Whether instantaneous effects captured; defaults FALSE. both_tails Set TRUE consider large extremely negative values. example, GARCH models, tails interest, VAR models, large values might relevant. p_value_based FALSE, Algorithm 1 used inferring edges. TRUE, testing procedure cut-p-value p_value_cutoff used detecting presence edge. procedures typically output similar results, testing procedure significantly slower. p_value_cutoff P-value cut-level reject absence edge estimated graph.

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph estimate — Extreme_causality_graph
text: named list containing: G graph defined edges. row corresponds edge first column pointing second column. Use graph <- graph_from_edgelist(G$G) igraph package obtain graph environment; weights Weights corresponding edge, representing close coefficient \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}\) 1. \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z} = 1\), weight 1. weight 0 \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z} = (1 + \hat{\Gamma}^{baseline}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}) / 2\).

what: Extreme Granger-causal graph parallelized estimate — Extreme_causality_graph_parallel
title: Extreme Granger-causal graph parallelized estimate — Extreme_causality_graph_parallel
text: function estimates causal graph (path diagram) set time series, using doFuture parallellization computational efficiency. Extreme_causality_graph(), potentially faster appropriate parallelization used.

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph parallelized estimate — Extreme_causality_graph_parallel
code: Extreme_causality_graph_parallel(   w,   max_causal_lag = 1,   max_confounder_lag = 0,   nu_x = 0.3,   q_y = 0.2,   q_z = 0.1,   instant = FALSE,   both_tails = TRUE,   p_value_based = FALSE,   p_value_cutoff = 0.05,   strategy = c("sequential", "multisession", "multicore", "mixed"),   n_workers = NULL )

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph parallelized estimate — Extreme_causality_graph_parallel
text: w data.frame time series, numeric length. max_causal_lag time delay effect x y. coefficient 'p' Appendix manuscript. max_confounder_lag lag \(Z\) \((X, Y)\). common cause different lags \(X\) \(Y\), may cause spurious causality \(X\) \(Y\). Ensure max_confounder_lag larger lag. nu_x coefficient \(\tau_X\) \(k_n\) manuscript, defined \(k_n = \lfloor n^{\nu_x} \rfloor\). strong hidden confounding expected, set nu_x 0.4 0.5. q_y coefficient \(\tau_y = q_y \times n\), describing conditioning \(Y_t\). large auto-correlation \(Y\), set q_y 0.1 less. Note manuscript, \(q_y\) defined 1 - q_y. q_z coefficient \(\tau_z = q_z \times n\), describing conditioning \(Z_t\). irrelevant z NULL. strong confounding effects, set q_z 0.2 0.3. Note manuscript, \(q_z\) defined 1 - q_z. instant Whether instantaneous effects captured; defaults FALSE. both_tails Set TRUE consider large extremely negative values. example, GARCH models, tails interest, VAR models, large values might relevant. p_value_based FALSE, Algorithm 1 used inferring edges. TRUE, testing procedure cut-p-value p_value_cutoff used detecting presence edge. procedures typically output similar results, testing procedure significantly slower. p_value_cutoff P-value cut-level reject absence edge estimated graph. strategy One "sequential" (default), "multisession", "multicore", "mixed". n_workers positive numeric scalar function specifying maximum number parallel futures can active time blocking. function, called without arguments future created value used configure workers. function return numeric scalar. Defaults future::availableCores()-1 NULL (default), "multicore" constraint relevant case. Ignored strategy=="sequential".

title: Extreme Granger-causal graph parallelized estimate — Extreme_causality_graph_parallel
text: named list containing: G graph defined edges. row corresponds edge first column pointing second column. Use graph <- graph_from_edgelist(G$G) igraph package obtain graph environment; weights Weights corresponding edge, representing close coefficient \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}\) 1. \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z} = 1\), weight 1. weight 0 \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z} = (1 + \hat{\Gamma}^{baseline}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}) / 2\).

title: Extreme Granger-causal test — Extreme_causality_test
text: function tests whether tails/extremes time series X cause time series Y, given potential confounders Z.

title: Extreme Granger-causal test — Extreme_causality_test
code: Extreme_causality_test(   x,   y,   z = NULL,   max_causal_lag = 1,   max_confounder_lag = 0,   nu_x = 0.3,   q_y = 0.2,   q_z = 0.1,   both_tails = TRUE,   instant = FALSE,   p_value_computation = FALSE,   bootstrap_repetitions = 50,   choice_of_F = 0.5 )

title: Extreme Granger-causal test — Extreme_causality_test
text: x numeric vector representing first time series (potential cause). y numeric vector representing second time series (potential effect). z data.frame potential confounders. Set NULL confounders. max_causal_lag time delay effect x y. coefficient 'p' Appendix manuscript. max_confounder_lag lag \(Z\) \((X, Y)\). common cause different lags \(X\) \(Y\), may cause spurious causality \(X\) \(Y\). Ensure max_confounder_lag larger lag. nu_x coefficient \(\tau_X\) \(k_n\) manuscript, defined \(k_n = \lfloor n^{\nu_x} \rfloor\). strong hidden confounding expected, set nu_x 0.4 0.5. q_y coefficient \(\tau_y = q_y \times n\), describing conditioning \(Y_t\). large auto-correlation \(Y\), set q_y 0.1 less. Note manuscript, \(q_y\) defined 1 - q_y. q_z coefficient \(\tau_z = q_z \times n\), describing conditioning \(Z_t\). irrelevant z NULL. strong confounding effects, set q_z 0.2 0.3. Note manuscript, \(q_z\) defined 1 - q_z. both_tails Set TRUE consider large extremely negative values. example, GARCH models, tails interest, VAR models, large values might relevant. instant Whether instantaneous effects captured; defaults FALSE. p_value_computation set FALSE faster "Algorithm 1" used. set TRUE p-value hypothesis \(H_0: X \text{ cause } Y \text{ extremes given } Z\) computed. p_value < 0.05, conclude \(X\) causes \(Y\) given \(Z\). bootstrap_repetitions number bootstrap repetitions p-value computation. repetitions yield precise p-values require longer computation time. choice_of_F Choice F coefficient. Leave default unless want reproduce results manuscript

title: Extreme Granger-causal test — Extreme_causality_test
text: named list containing: is_causal logical value indicating whether evidence causality detected; output Either 'Evidence causality' 'causality' based Algorithm 1 manuscript; p_value_tail shown p_value_computation==FALSE. Rejection indicates evidence causality tail. corresponds p-value hypothesis H_0: "X cause Y tail given Z", based bootstrapping. Often p_value==1 means CTC<baseline; p_value_extreme shown p_value_computation==FALSE. Rejection indicates evidence causality extremes. corresponds p-value hypothesis H_0: \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z} < (1 + 3 \cdot \hat{\Gamma}^{baseline}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}) / 4\); CTC coefficient \(\hat{\Gamma}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}\); baseline baseline coefficient \(\hat{\Gamma}^{baseline}_{X\rightarrow Y | Z}\).
